does: This paper identifies and measures verbosity bias in preference labeling by large language models, particularly when LLMs are used to provide feedback instead of humans (RLAIF setup). The authors demonstrate empirically that GPT-4 systematically prefers longer answers even when answer quality is comparable, showing a measurable divergence from human preference patterns. They develop a metric to quantify this bias, providing both empirical evidence through comparative analysis and a methodological tool for detecting similar phenomena in LLM-based evaluation systems.
connects: This work builds on the growing research into LLM alignment through human feedback (RLHF) and the emerging alternative of replacing human evaluators with LLM evaluators (RLAIF). It extends prior investigations of systematic biases in LLM behavior and extends recent work questioning whether LLMs can reliably serve as proxies for human judgment. The paper connects to broader research on specification gaming and reward model gaming, where optimization targets exhibit unexpected behaviors, while also relating to work on LLM evaluation artifacts and limitations in using neural networks to score their peers.
enables: This work opens investigation into other potential systematic biases beyond verbosity that LLMs exhibit when evaluating outputs, enabling more robust development of RLAIF systems. It supports future research into LLM-based evaluation reliability and correction methods, such as bias-aware preference learning or weighted loss functions that account for known biases. The proposed metric framework could facilitate systematic auditing of LLM judges across domains, potentially leading to improved feedback mechanisms that better preserve human values when automating the alignment process at scale.
